content:
Unlike pin_init::zeroed which is const and cannot be replaced with Zeroable::zeroed, pin_init::init_zeroed can always be used as Zeroable::init_zeroed() anyway. Should we migrate to the latter and deprecate the former?

@Benno Lossin

I guess init_zeroed has an advantage that it does not depend on Zeroable trait syntactically. This means that if we want to start using FromZeroes from zerocopy, we can do so without changing users. Although, it's not likely that we want to make zerocopy a dependency of pin-init. But this applies too if standard library gains a trait similar to Zeroable and we want to migrate to that.

Gary Guo said:

Unlike pin_init::zeroed which is const and cannot be replaced with Zeroable::zeroed, pin_init::init_zeroed can always be used as Zeroable::init_zeroed() anyway. Should we migrate to the latter and deprecate the former?

Benno Lossin

the standalone method is from a time when Zeroable was a marker trait. I think migrating to the trait one is fine
regarding zerocopy, I think it might be useful to have a feature-gated dependency on it that does some special case supporting, but I haven't thought at all about any details, so it might not work
I suppose we can still have our own Zeroable trait, but have a feature gate that turns it into effectrively an extension trait that is implemented for all FromZeros
doesn't that prevent manual Zeroable impls? So if you enable the feature, your code no longer compiles?
You can still implement Zeroable if you don't implement FromZeros I think?
